findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 43. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: Ensure the main landmark is at top level
findings.evidence:
Fix any of the following:
  The main landmark is contained in another landmark. [<main>]

findings.description: Ensure the document has at most one main landmark
findings.evidence:
Fix any of the following:
  Document has more than one main landmark [<main>]

Fix any of the following:
  The landmark must have a unique aria-label, aria-labelledby, or title to make landmarks distinguishable [<main>]

findings.description: The NC Energy, Mineral & Land Resources logo/graphic in the PDF header is not present in the HTML conversion.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows the NC logo with tree graphic in the upper left. HTML page equivalent shows the organization header text and contact info but rendered differently as a banner-style header without the logo graphic.

findings.description: Page 2 in the PDF is essentially a blank page with only the letterhead header. The HTML does not reproduce this blank page, which is acceptable for pagination but noted.
findings.evidence: PDF page 2 shows only the NC Energy, Mineral & Land Resources letterhead with no content. The HTML skips this entirely and continues with content.

findings.description: The memo header fields (To, From, Subject) are rendered as a table in the HTML but as simple labeled text lines in the PDF. While the data is preserved, the addition of table borders/structure changes the visual presentation significantly.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'To:', 'From:', 'Subject:' as simple indented text lines. HTML renders these in a bordered table with cells.

findings.description: In Section II, the outline numbering for the report sections uses Roman numerals with periods in the PDF but appears with periods and as hyperlinks in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'I. History/Background' etc. as plain text. HTML shows them as clickable hyperlinks with underline styling.

findings.description: The bullet style difference in Section I History/Background: PDF uses dash/dot bullets while HTML uses filled circle bullets.
findings.evidence: PDF page 3 uses small dot/dash bullets for the CAMA process list items. HTML uses standard filled circle bullets.

findings.description: The HTML adds internal anchor links in the table of contents that do not exist in the PDF. The PDF simply lists the outline items without hyperlinks.
findings.evidence: HTML contains <a href="#history-background">, <a href="#march-24-2016-public-meeting-and-oral-comments-summary">, etc. These are navigational additions not present in the source PDF, though they point to content that does exist in the document.

findings.description: The HTML has a nested <main> element inside another <main>, which is a structural issue but does not affect content fidelity.
findings.evidence: The HTML body contains <main> wrapping the entire content, and then another <main> element inside it after the <header>.

findings.description: The memo header (To/From/Subject) is rendered as a table in HTML, but it is not a table in the PDF - it is formatted as a standard memo header with labels and values. This is an acceptable structural interpretation but noted for completeness. The table includes a comment '<!-- COLUMNS: Field | Value -->' which is metadata not in the PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF shows standard memo format; HTML wraps it in <table> with comment <!-- COLUMNS: Field | Value -->
